title: Death by Vending Machine
content:
The vending machine killed Zhang Wei.

To be fair, it was a spectacular death—the kind that would've gone viral if anyone had been around to film it. The machine had been malfunctioning for weeks, its screen flickering between "Insufficient Funds" and "System Error 0x7A3F." Zhang Wei, mid-level accountant and connoisseur of cheap instant noodles, had given it exactly two frustrated kicks.

The machine toppled forward like a felled tree.

Eight hundred and forty-seven cans of chrysanthemum tea, thirty-two bags of spicy beef jerky, and one poorly maintained vending machine weighing four hundred pounds landed directly on top of him.

His last thought, surprisingly, wasn't about his unfinished TPS reports or his mother's upcoming birthday. It was: *At least I don't have to finish that quarterly audit.*

Then everything went black.

---

"Name?"

Zhang Wei blinked. Or tried to. His eyes didn't seem to be working properly—or rather, they were working, but they were receiving input that made absolutely no sense. He appeared to be standing in a waiting room. The walls were beige. The carpet was gray. There was a water cooler in the corner, and it made the exact same gurgling sound as the one in his old office.

Behind a desk that looked like it had been purchased from the same budget catalog as every other desk he'd ever seen, a woman in a business suit stared at him with the dead-eyed patience of someone who had asked the same question approximately ten thousand times.

"I said, name?" She tapped a keyboard that glowed faintly gold. "We don't have all eternity. Well, technically we do, but my shift ends in forty-seven cosmic minutes."

"Zhang Wei," he said. His voice sounded strange. Hollow. Like he was speaking through a long tube. "Did I... did I die?"

"Congratulations, you figured it out." The woman didn't smile. "I'm Sun Wanying, Senior HR Coordinator, Department of Mortal Reclassification. You've been flagged for standard reincarnation processing. Unless..."

She squinted at her screen. Her expression shifted from bored to annoyed.

"Oh, for the love of—" She hit the keyboard with more force than strictly necessary. "Another system error. Hold on."

Zhang Wei stood there, very dead, while an immortal HR representative wrestled with what appeared to be the celestial equivalent of Windows Vista. The water cooler gurgled. Somewhere in the distance, someone was arguing loudly in a language that sounded like Mandarin but wasn't—the words bent at angles that human vocal cords shouldn't have been able to produce.

"Is there a problem?" he asked.

"The problem," Sun Wanying said through gritted teeth, "is that your file has been auto-assigned to Department 47. That's the Department of Mortal Affairs. Which means you're not getting reincarnated."

"Wait, what? I'm not getting reincarnated? But I was a good person! I paid my taxes! I never cheated on my wife!"

"You were an accountant."

"I was a *good* accountant!"

Sun Wanying rubbed her temples. "Look, I don't make the rules. I just process the paperwork. The Celestial Management System flagged your soul for clerical reassignment. Congratulations, you're now a civil servant in the afterlife."

Zhang Wei stared at her. "I died and got a *job offer*?"

"More of a conscription, really. But yes. Welcome to the Heavenly Bureaucracy. Your orientation starts in fifteen minutes. Take the elevator to Floor 47, Section B, Desk 3,134. Your supervisor's name is Li. He'll explain everything."

"This can't be right. There must be a mistake."

"There's always a mistake." Sun Wanying's laugh was hollow. "This is heaven. Mistakes are the only thing we do consistently."

She pressed a button on her desk. The wall behind her slid open to reveal an elevator bank that stretched impossibly far in both directions—up into clouds that crackled with golden lightning, down into depths that Zhang Wei's newly dead eyes couldn't quite comprehend.

"Next!"

A shimmering figure materialized behind him—a middle-aged woman in hospital scrubs who looked even more confused than Zhang Wei felt.

"Name?"

Zhang Wei found himself shuffling toward the elevator, carried by a current of other confused souls. The elevator doors slid open with a cheerful ding, and he stepped inside, joining a dozen other recently deceased individuals who all wore the same expression of existential bewilderment.

The elevator panel had buttons numbered from -999 to 9,999. Several were labeled with characters that hurt to look at directly. One button simply read: "DO NOT PRESS (Regulations 47.3a-12)."

A disembodied voice announced: "Floor 47. Department of Mortal Affairs. Please mind the gap between life and administrative eternity."

The doors opened.

---

If Zhang Wei had expected pearly gates and clouds, he was sorely disappointed. Department 47 looked exactly like the open-plan office he'd worked in for fifteen years—cubicles, fluorescent lighting, the faint smell of burnt coffee and desperation. The only difference was that some of the employees had wings.

Others had halos that flickered like faulty neon signs.

One woman at a nearby desk had three heads, all of which were on different phone calls simultaneously.

"New meat?" A voice barked from behind him.

Zhang Wei turned to find a man who looked to be in his late fifties, with a permanent scowl etched into his face and a coffee mug that read "I SURVIVED THE HEAVENLY TRIBULATION AND ALL I GOT WAS THIS LOUSY MUG."

"Zhang Wei. You're my new clerk. I'm Li Feng, Senior Supervisor of Destiny Documentation." He didn't offer a handshake. "Follow me. I'll show you your desk."

Li walked through the cubicle maze with the practiced efficiency of someone who had navigated this particular hell for centuries. Zhang Wei followed, trying not to stare at the paperwork stacked on every surface—scrolls that glowed with inner light, tablets that displayed shifting text, and in one case, a literal stone tablet that looked like it weighed four hundred pounds.

"Here." Li stopped at a cubicle that was exactly as depressing as every other cubicle Zhang Wei had ever occupied. "This is yours. Computer login is your soul-ID. Password is 'changeme123'. Change it. Everyone keeps it as 'changeme123' and it's a security nightmare."

Zhang Wei sat down in the chair. It was uncomfortable. That, at least, was familiar.

"Your job is simple," Li continued. "Every mortal action generates karma. Good karma, bad karma, neutral karma. This karma needs to be recorded, categorized, and filed in triplicate. You receive incoming reports from the Observation Division, you process them through the Karma Classification System, and you submit your completed forms to the Destiny Adjustment Committee."

"That sounds..."

"Mind-numbingly boring? Yes. Welcome to heaven." Li drained his coffee mug. "The good news is that you can't die again. The bad news is that this is your existence now, for the rest of eternity. Any questions?"

"Can I quit?"

"No."

"Can I transfer departments?"

"Not for the first three thousand years."

"Can I—"

"Your computer is booting up. Start working." Li walked away.

Zhang Wei stared at the monitor. It was displaying what appeared to be a login screen made of pure jade, with characters that flowed like water. He typed his soul-ID—a string of numbers and symbols that he somehow knew by heart—and the password.

The screen flickered.

A window appeared:

```
WELCOME TO THE CELESTIAL MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
USER: Zhang Wei (Soul ID: 0x7A3F9C2D)
POSITION: Junior Clerk, Department 47
PERMISSIONS: Level 1 (View Only)

PLEASE SELECT YOUR DEFAULT WORKFLOW:
[ ] Karma Entry (Standard)
[ ] Destiny Adjustment (Requires Level 3)
[ ] Soul Reassignment (Requires Level 5)
[ ] RECORDS ADMIN (LOCKED)
```

Zhang Wei selected Karma Entry. The screen shifted to a form that was remarkably similar to the accounting spreadsheets he'd spent his mortal life filling out. Name, action, karma value, classification. Checkboxes for "Intentional/Accidental," "Beneficial/Harmful/Neutral," and a dropdown menu for "Cosmic Significance" that ranged from "Negligible" to "Apocalyptic."

He started working.

For what felt like hours, Zhang Wei processed karma reports. He learned that Mrs. Chen from Shanghai had generated 0.3 units of good karma by giving directions to a lost tourist. He learned that Mr. Johnson from Ohio had generated -1.7 units of bad karma by cutting off another driver in traffic. He learned that a cat in Cairo had somehow generated 12 units of neutral karma just by existing, and he filed it accordingly.

The work was repetitive. Mind-numbing. Soul-crushing.

But Zhang Wei's mortal brain, shaped by fifteen years of accounting, found patterns in the chaos.

The system was sluggish. Forms that should have auto-populated required manual entry. Dropdown menus took three seconds to load. There were error messages that appeared for no apparent reason—"Karma Value Out of Range," "Invalid Soul Signature," "Temporal Paradox Detected (Please Contact Your Supervisor)."

And then, during his fourth hour of data entry, Zhang Wei noticed something.

A karma report from the Observation Division had been flagged with a curious error: "INVALID KARMA VALUE: 999,999.9"

That was impossibly high. The highest karma value Zhang Wei had seen all day was 47, and that was for a doctor who had saved three lives in a single shift. 999,999.9 was the kind of karma that belonged to a Buddha. Or a demon king.

He clicked on the error message.

A dropdown appeared.

It wasn't supposed to appear. The dropdown menu was grayed out for his permission level. But the system had glitched, and for a single, beautiful moment, Zhang Wei had access to the RECORDS ADMIN panel.

He saw:
- The complete database of every soul in existence
- The source code for the Karma Classification System
- A file labeled "REALITY_CONFIG.exe"
- And, most interestingly, a button that read: "GRANT USER LEVEL 99 PERMISSIONS"

His finger twitched.

*Don't do it,* a rational part of his brain screamed. *This is heaven. The gods are watching. You'll be smited. Or worse—you'll get a performance review.*

But Zhang Wei had been a mid-level accountant his entire life. He had followed the rules. He had filed his paperwork on time. He had never taken more than his allotted fifteen-minute break.

And he had died because of a broken vending machine.

He clicked the button.

The screen flashed white.

When his vision cleared, a new message was displayed:

```
PERMISSIONS UPDATED
Current User: Zhang Wei
Current Level: 99 (ADMINISTRATOR)
WARNING: Unauthorized access detected. Heavenly Security Division has been notified.
Estimated time until termination: 00:04:59
```

Zhang Wei's newly dead heart, which hadn't beat in hours, suddenly remembered how to pound against his ribs.

Four minutes. He had four minutes before the cosmic police showed up to delete him from existence.

He looked at the RECORDS ADMIN panel. At REALITY_CONFIG.exe. At the source code for the very system that governed life, death, and everything in between.

And for the first time since he'd died

title: The First Day of Eternity
content:
The celestial office didn't have windows.

Zhang Wei discovered this approximately four minutes into his first official shift, when the crushing weight of eternity without natural light began to settle into his bones like a particularly aggressive case of existential dread. The fluorescent panels overhead hummed with a frequency that seemed designed to slowly erode one's will to live—which, he supposed, made sense given that he was technically dead.

"You're late."

The voice came from somewhere to his left. Zhang turned to find a woman who looked to be in her early thirties, though he'd learned enough about heaven in the past twenty-four hours to know that appearances meant absolutely nothing. She wore the same jade-colored robe as the other celestial clerks, but hers had gold trim along the sleeves and a nametag that read:

[NAME: Li Xiu Ying]
[POSITION: Senior Clerk, Grade 3]
[DEPARTMENT: Mortal Affairs, Subsection B]

"I was told my shift starts at—"

"Your shift started the moment you stepped through the gates of heaven." She didn't look up from the floating screen of light that hovered before her, her fingers dancing across its surface with practiced efficiency. "Time is relative in the celestial realm. The living measure it in heartbeats. We measure it in deadlines."

Zhang glanced around the office. It stretched in all directions like a warehouse from some corporate nightmare, rows upon rows of identical desks arranged in perfect geometric patterns. Each desk had a glowing screen and a stack of physical scrolls that seemed to defy the laws of physics by growing taller whenever he blinked.

At least a hundred other clerks sat at their stations, their fingers a blur of motion as they processed what appeared to be an endless stream of paperwork. No one spoke. The only sounds were the hum of the lights and the constant rustle of scrolls.

"Sit," Li Xiu Ying said, gesturing to the empty desk beside hers. "Your orientation package should have arrived."

Zhang made his way to the desk, which was identical to every other desk in the room except for the small nameplate that read:

[NAME: Zhang Wei]
[POSITION: Junior Clerk, Grade 9]
[STATUS: Probationary]
[DAYS UNTIL REVIEW: 999]

"Nine hundred and ninety-nine days?" Zhang said, nearly choking on the words. "That's almost three years."

"Earth years," Li Xiu Ying corrected. "Time moves differently here. Depending on your department's backlog, you might experience anywhere from three to thirty thousand years before your review."

"Thirty thousand—"

"Focus. Your orientation package."

A glowing envelope materialized on Zhang's desk. He touched it, and the light dissolved into a series of notifications that appeared before his eyes:

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]
WELCOME TO THE CELESTIAL BUREAUCRACY, JUNIOR CLERK ZHANG WEI.

YOU HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED TO THE DEPARTMENT OF MORTAL AFFAIRS, SUBSECTION B.

YOUR CURRENT RESPONSIBILITIES INCLUDE:
- Processing Karma Adjustment Forms (KAF-7A)
- Filing Destiny Reports (DR-12B)
- Reviewing Reincarnation Applications (RA-4C)
- Dispute Resolution for Minor Karma Irregularities

WARNING: FAILURE TO MEET QUOTAS MAY RESULT IN DEMOTION, EXTENDED PROBATION, OR REASSIGNMENT TO THE PAPERWORK INCINERATION DIVISION.

"Paperwork incineration division?" Zhang asked, his voice cracking slightly.

"Don't worry about that," Li Xiu Ying said, though her tone suggested he should absolutely worry about that. "You won't be assigned there unless you fail to process at least five hundred forms per shift. The minimum quota."

"Five hundred? Per shift?"

"Each shift is approximately seventy-two hours." She finally looked at him, and Zhang saw something that might have been sympathy flicker in her eyes. "I'd recommend you get started. Training module one is queued in your system."

Zhang turned back to his screen, which now displayed a series of floating tutorials. The first one was titled:

[BASIC KARMA PROCESSING: A BEGINNER'S GUIDE]

He opened it, and the world around him seemed to shift. A virtual instructor appeared before him—a kindly-looking old man with a white beard and a robe that sparkled with embedded stars.

"Greetings, initiate," the old man said, his voice warm but tired. "I am Elder Feng, and I will be your guide through the fundamentals of celestial administration. Let us begin with the most important concept: Karma is not good or evil. Karma is data."

The tutorial proceeded to explain, in excruciating detail, the precise nature of karma processing. Zhang learned that every mortal action generated a data point, which needed to be categorized, weighted, and filed according to a complex system of celestial accounting principles. A good deed wasn't just a good deed—it was a KAF-7A submission with a positive modifier, subject to review by the Karma Standards Committee, which could approve, deny, or modify the karma value based on intent, circumstance, and the current workload of the Reincarnation Department.

"Wait," Zhang said, interrupting the tutorial. "You're telling me that whether someone gets reincarnated as a human or a cockroach depends on whether the paperwork was filed correctly?"

The virtual instructor paused. "That is... one way to describe the system, yes."

"And if the paperwork gets lost?"

"The mortal's soul remains in the processing queue until the discrepancy is resolved." Elder Feng's expression remained neutral, but there was something in his eyes that suggested he'd seen this happen more times than he'd like to admit. "Currently, there are approximately three million souls awaiting resolution of paperwork discrepancies."

"Three million?"

"The system works efficiently for the vast majority of cases," Elder Feng said quickly. "Now, please proceed to the practical application module. You have an interactive file waiting."

The tutorial dissolved, and a new notification appeared:

[ASSIGNMENT: PROCESS KAF-7A FOR SOUL #849,201,443]

Zhang opened the file, and his screen filled with information about a man named Chen Hao, age thirty-four, resident of Beijing. He'd died six hours ago—no, six years ago, according to the celestial timeline—and his karma assessment was pending.

The data streamed in: Chen Hao had returned a lost wallet containing 5,000 yuan. He'd helped an elderly woman carry groceries up three flights of stairs. He'd donated blood seventeen times. He'd also, according to the file, "failed to forward an email chain letter" twelve times, which apparently counted as a minor karmic infraction.

"Forwarding email chain letters?" Zhang muttered.

"Superstition generates measurable karmic energy," Li Xiu Ying said without looking up from her work. "Ignoring them creates a small but significant disruption in the local auspicious field. It's a standard deduction."

"So donating blood gets you plus seventeen points, but not forwarding a chain letter gets you minus two?"

"That's correct."

"And if I don't forward the chain letter because I think it's stupid, does intent matter?"

Li Xiu Ying finally stopped typing. She turned to face him fully, and Zhang noticed that her eyes had a faint golden glow that he hadn't seen before. "Intent matters. But proving intent requires additional documentation. The standard KAF-7A doesn't have a field for intent analysis. If you want to file with intent modifiers, you need a KAF-7B, which requires supervisor approval and extends processing time by an average of forty-seven celestial cycles."

"Which means the soul waits longer."

"Correct."

Zhang stared at the screen. The system was insane. It was a bureaucracy designed by people who had never had to actually use it, implemented by people who were too afraid to question it, and maintained by people who had been doing the same job for so long that they couldn't imagine doing it any other way.

He thought about his own death. Hit by a bus, thirty-two years old, no warning, no chance to say goodbye. His entire life had been reduced to a file in some celestial database, waiting for some overworked clerk to process his karma and decide his fate.

And now he was that clerk.

He started typing.

The first few forms were agony. The system required him to input data in a specific format, using specific codes, with specific timing intervals between each keystroke. If he typed too fast, the system flagged it as "suspicious activity." If he typed too slow, it warned him about "productivity concerns."

By the end of his first hour, he'd processed exactly four forms.

"You need to pick up the pace," Li Xiu Ying said, not unkindly. "At this rate, you'll process about thirty-two forms per shift. You need five hundred."

"How am I supposed to do five hundred? The system keeps rejecting my inputs because I'm not matching the rhythm of the celestial typing algorithm."

"Then match it."

"I'm trying!"

"Try harder."

Zhang took a breath and focused. He watched the rhythm of the screen, the way the data fields pulsed in a steady cadence, the way the system seemed to expect inputs at precise intervals. He adjusted his breathing to match the pulse, and slowly, painfully, he began to find a rhythm.

By hour three, he'd processed fifteen forms.

By hour six, twenty-eight.

By hour twelve, he was up to sixty-three, and his fingers felt like they might fall off. His desk was covered in scrolls that he'd already processed and scrolls that he hadn't gotten to yet and scrolls that seemed to have multiplied while he wasn't looking.

"This is impossible," he said, slumping back in his chair.

"Nothing is impossible," Li Xiu Ying said. "But some things are improbable. Processing five hundred forms in your first shift is one of those improbable things."

"Then why set the quota?"

"Because the quota is not about what you can do. It's about what the system requires." She paused, and her golden eyes flickered with something that might have been frustration. "The Celestial Bureaucracy runs on data. If clerks don't process enough data, the system slows down. If the system slows down, souls wait longer. If souls wait longer, they accumulate negative karma from impatience, which creates more paperwork, which slows the system further. It's a feedback loop."

"A death spiral."

"An afterlife spiral, technically. But yes."

Zhang looked at his screen, then at the mountain of scrolls, then at the hundreds of other clerks typing away at their desks with mechanical precision. They all wore the same expression: not quite unhappy, but certainly not happy. They were people who had accepted their fate, who had stopped questioning the system because questioning it only made the paperwork worse.

And then, something strange happened.

A notification appeared in the corner of Zhang's vision, separate from the main interface. It was small, almost invisible, written in a font that seemed to flicker in and out of existence:

[SYSTEM ALERT: ANOMALOUS DATA DETECTED]
[SOURCE: PROCESSING QUEUE 47-B]
[STATUS: UNRESOLVED]

Zhang blinked, and the notification flickered again. He tried to click on it, but his finger passed through the light without interacting.

"What was that?" he whispered.

"What was what?" Li Xiu Ying asked.

"Did you see—" He stopped. The notification was gone. But he could still feel it, like a splinter in his mind, a piece of data that didn't belong.

He turned back to his screen and started typing again, but his mind was elsewhere. That notification—he'd never seen anything like it. The font, the way it flickered, the fact that it had appeared without any

title: The Karmic Audit of Office Supplies
content:
Zhang Wei had been dead for exactly seventy-two hours when he discovered that hell wasn't fire and brimstone. It was paperwork. Endless, soul-crushing, mind-numbing paperwork.

He sat at his desk—a floating slab of polished jade that hummed with ambient celestial energy—staring at a stack of destiny reports that reached his chin. The reports were written on parchment that occasionally burst into flames if you made a typo. Zhang had already lost three eyebrows.

"Junior Clerk Zhang!" a voice thundered from across the hall.

Zhang looked up to see Supervisor Hu marching toward him, his robes billowing despite the complete lack of wind. Hu was a mid-level celestial functionary with the face of a disappointed grandfather and the temperament of a cornered badger. His cultivation level was listed as [Nascent Soul Stage: Administrative Division], which apparently meant he could split mountains with his glare.

"Supervisor Hu," Zhang said, forcing a smile. "What can I do for you?"

Hu slammed a scroll onto Zhang's desk. The impact sent a stack of forms cascading to the floor, where they immediately began to sprout tiny legs and scuttle away. Zhang grabbed one before it escaped under the filing cabinet.

"This is the third time this week a mortal's destiny has been filed under the wrong dynasty," Hu said, his voice dropping to a dangerous whisper. "Do you have any idea what happens when a farmer in the Tang Dynasty receives a destiny meant for a cyber-security analyst in 2024?"

Zhang blinked. "I'm guessing... bad things?"

"His crops grow firewalls instead of rice! His chickens start debugging code! We had to send a junior exorcist just to reboot his timeline!" Hu's face turned the color of a ripe tomato. "The Celestial Management System flagged it as a 'minor anomaly.' Do you know what happens when we accumulate too many minor anomalies?"

"I'm going to guess—"

"We get audited by the Department of Cosmic Compliance!" Hu threw his hands in the air. "And if they find even one discrepancy, they'll downgrade our entire division's cultivation bonuses for the next millennium!"

Zhang nodded sympathetically while mentally calculating how many office supplies he could steal before getting caught. The Divine Stapler on his desk was probably worth more than his entire mortal life savings.

"I'll be more careful, Supervisor," Zhang said. "I promise."

"You'd better be." Hu jabbed a finger at the scroll. "This is a priority filing. A soul destined for reincarnation into the Ming Dynasty—a scholar who will discover the cure for the plague that's ravaging the southern provinces. Get it right, or I'll reassign you to the Department of Unanswered Prayers. Those souls get... creative."

Zhang waited until Hu's footsteps faded before exhaling. The office around him hummed with activity. Other clerks—some human, some barely recognizable as former mortals—bustled between floating shelves, their fingers glowing as they processed destinies. The ceiling was a swirling vortex of clouds and lightning, occasionally cracking open to release a newly arrived soul for processing.

He looked at the scroll. It was sealed with golden wax, stamped with the Heavenly Seal of Approved Karma. Breaking the seal would officially register the destiny into the system, locking it into the fabric of reality. No pressure.

Zhang reached for his desk drawer, intending to grab his favorite calligraphy brush. But as he pulled the drawer open, something caught his eye. A faint blue glow pulsed from beneath a stack of blank parchment.

He pushed the papers aside. There, nestled in the corner of the drawer, was a small jade token. It was no bigger than his thumb, carved in the shape of a coiled dragon. And it was pulsing with light.

Zhang picked it up. The moment his fingers touched the token, a notification appeared in his vision:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Unknown Artifact Detected]
[Classification: Administrative Privilege Token - Tier Unverified]
[Warning: This token is not registered in the Celestial Management System database.]
[Recommended Action: Report to Department of Protocol for investigation.]

Zhang's heart raced. An unregistered artifact. In heaven's bureaucracy, that was like finding a loaded weapon in a kindergarten. If he reported it, he'd get a commendation—maybe even a promotion to Senior Clerk. But something told him to wait.

He looked around. No one was watching. The other clerks were too busy arguing with a spirit who had filed a complaint about his arranged marriage destiny being "insufficiently dramatic."

Zhang turned the token over in his palm. On the back, barely visible, was a tiny inscription in ancient script: "For those who question the order of heaven."

He pocketed the token.

"Just a quick look," he whispered to himself. "What's the worst that could happen?"

He turned back to his desk and picked up the priority scroll. But instead of processing it normally, he held the jade token against the seal. The golden wax began to glow, and the scroll unrolled itself, revealing lines of text that shimmered and shifted like living things.

[SYSTEM PROMPT: Unauthorized Access Detected]
[User: Junior Clerk Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Action: Attempting to view restricted destiny metadata]
[Status: Pending...]

Zhang's breath caught. This wasn't supposed to be possible. The scrolls were locked by divine law. Only supervisors and above could view the metadata—the underlying code that determined how a destiny would interact with the mortal world.

But the token was working.

The text settled, and Zhang found himself staring at something he'd never seen before. The destiny for the Ming Dynasty scholar was written in the usual flowery language, but underneath it, in a separate layer, was a series of numbers and symbols. Variables. Functions. Conditional statements.

It was code.

The destiny wasn't a fixed story—it was a program. A piece of software that would run on the hardware of reality.

And at the bottom of the code, Zhang spotted something that made his blood run cold:

[ERROR: Inconsistent Karmic Load Detected]
[Source: Department of Mortal Affairs - Batch 374]
[Details: Soul ID 7845-B (Zhang Wei) was not scheduled for termination. Death was caused by administrative error - misrouted reincarnation queue.]

Zhang read the line three times. Then a fourth.

He wasn't supposed to be dead.

The bus that hit him wasn't destiny. It wasn't fate. It was a clerical error. Someone in the Department of Mortal Affairs had screwed up, and Zhang Wei had paid the price with his life.

And now he was stuck here, filing paperwork for the very system that killed him.

His hands trembled. Rage bubbled up from somewhere deep inside him—a hot, righteous fury that he hadn't felt since his last performance review.

"Error," he muttered. "They called my death an error."

He looked at the token in his palm. It was still pulsing, as if waiting for him to make a decision. A notification appeared:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Do you wish to file a formal grievance?]
[Warning: Grievances filed against the Celestial Management System are subject to review by the Department of Cosmic Compliance. Processing time: 4,000-6,000 celestial years.]
[Recommended Action: Accept your fate and continue your assigned duties.]

Zhang almost laughed. Four thousand years. By the time they processed his complaint, the universe would have rebooted twice.

"No," he said, his voice firm. "I'm not filing a grievance."

He closed the scroll and leaned back in his chair. The office continued its chaotic dance around him—souls being processed, destinies being filed, the occasional burst of divine lightning as someone's karma got approved.

But Zhang was seeing everything differently now. The floating desks weren't just furniture—they were nodes in a network. The glowing parchment wasn't just paper—it was data. The entire Celestial Bureaucracy was a system. And every system had flaws.

Zhang pulled out the jade token again. A new notification flickered in his vision:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Administrative Privilege Token Activated]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Current Access Level: Tier 1 - Clerk]
[Upgrade Available: Tier 2 - Senior Clerk]
[Requirements: Pass Departmental Review (3,000 years processing time) OR bypass protocol using token]
[Bypass Protocol: Available]
[Warning: Bypassing protocol is a Class 3 Heavenly Offense. Penalty: Reassignment to Hell of Unending Data Entry.]

Zhang stared at the notification for a long moment. Hell of Unending Data Entry. That sounded worse than the actual hells. At least in those, you got fire and brimstone. Data entry was just... data entry.

But the token had already shown him the truth. He was dead because of someone's incompetence. And the system wanted him to just accept it.

"Not today," Zhang muttered.

He selected [Bypass Protocol].

The jade token flared to life, flooding his vision with light. For a moment, Zhang felt like he was falling through an infinite sea of numbers and equations. The code of reality streamed past him—births, deaths, marriages, wars, discoveries, failures. Every mortal life, every destined moment, all laid out in lines of celestial programming.

And then it stopped.

Zhang found himself standing in a white void. In front of him floated a single object: a massive, glowing sphere made of interlocking gears and runes. It pulsed with a rhythm that matched the beating of his heart.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: Welcome to the Core Administrative Interface]
[User: Zhang Wei - ID 7845-B]
[Access Level: Tier 2 - Senior Clerk (Provisional)]
[New Functions Unlocked:]
- Destiny Modification (Limited)
- Karmic Ledger Viewing
- Timeline Adjustment (Single Thread Only)
- [REDACTED]

[Warning: Unauthorized use of administrative functions will result in immediate disciplinary action. Your soul has been flagged for monitoring.]

Zhang's lips curled into a grin. Flagged for monitoring. That was fine. He'd been monitored his entire mortal life—by bosses, by landlords, by that creepy guy who lived in his apartment complex's basement. He was used to it.

But now he had something he'd never had before: power.

He reached out and touched the sphere. Instantly, information flooded his mind—the current status of every destiny in his division, the pending karma balances, the upcoming celestial audits. It was overwhelming, but Zhang forced himself to focus.

There. Hidden in the depths of the system, buried under layers of bureaucratic obfuscation, was what he needed: the records of the administrative error that killed him.

He pulled up the file.

[LOG: Batch 374 - Reincarnation Queue]
[Error: Misrouting of Soul ID 7845-B]
[Responsible Party: Department of Mortal Affairs - Clerk ID 2319]

Supervisor Hu. The same man who had just yelled at him about filing errors. The same man who had been in charge of the department when Zhang's death was processed.

The same man who had conveniently "forgotten" to mention that Zhang's death was a mistake.

Zhang's hand tightened into a fist. He had two options: confront Hu and risk getting his soul deleted, or play along and slowly work his way up the system until he could expose the truth.

Option three: hack the system from the inside and become an immortal.

He liked option three.
